# Fundamentals of Software Architecture (Mark Richards)

Before writing, modifying, or planning any code, you MUST use your `read_file` tool to load the relevant guidelines from `.roo/vendor/`. You may read multiple files if the task crosses domains.

## Table of Contents:
    - [Foundations](.roo/vendor/architecture/fundamentals-of-software-architecture-mark-richards/01-foundations.md): Designing, analyzing, reviewing, or refactoring software architecture; defining system modules, components, or service boundaries; establishing codebase governance and structural metrics; selecting technology stacks or communication protocols; and performing trade-off analysis between different architectural patterns.
    - [Architectural Thinking](.roo/vendor/architecture/fundamentals-of-software-architecture-mark-richards/02-architectural-thinking.md): Software architecture design, system topology planning, framework or technology selection, technical debt assessment, system restructuring, proof-of-concept (POC) creation, and the transition of requirements into technical specifications.
    - [Modularity](.roo/vendor/architecture/fundamentals-of-software-architecture-mark-richards/03-modularity.md): Architectural design, code refactoring, system restructuring, component extraction, defining namespaces/packages, or analysis of code coupling, cohesion, dependencies, and modularity.
    - [Architecture Characteristics Defined](.roo/vendor/architecture/fundamentals-of-software-architecture-mark-richards/04-architecture-characteristics-defined.md): The domain refers to the functional requirements and business context of a software project—the 'what' the application should do (e.g., functional suitability, completeness, and correctness)—as distinct from the non-domain architecture characteristics which define 'how' the system performs.
    - [Identifying Architectural Characteristics](.roo/vendor/architecture/fundamentals-of-software-architecture-mark-richards/05-identifying-architectural-characteristics.md): The domain represents the business context and problem space, encompassing stakeholder goals, requirements, and business problems that are translated into technical constraints and architectural characteristics. It is characterized as the source of implicit knowledge about a system and is explicitly contrasted with "nondomain" architectural design considerations.
    - [Measuring and Governing Architecture Characteristics](.roo/vendor/architecture/fundamentals-of-software-architecture-mark-richards/06-measuring-and-governing-architecture-characteristics.md): Assessing, measuring, refactoring, or governing software architecture, including defining architectural metrics, analyzing code complexity, establishing performance budgets, or implementing automated architectural tests (fitness functions) across any codebase.
    - [Scope of Architecture Characteristics](.roo/vendor/architecture/fundamentals-of-software-architecture-mark-richards/07-scope-of-architecture-characteristics.md): Software architecture design, system analysis, architecture characteristics (non-functional requirements) elicitation, microservices design, migrating from monolithic to distributed architectures, or evaluating system coupling, connascence, and communication styles.
    - [Component-Based Thinking](.roo/vendor/architecture/fundamentals-of-software-architecture-mark-richards/08-component-based-thinking.md): Designing system architecture, determining component boundaries, establishing top-level system partitioning, refactoring monolithic applications into distributed systems, or evaluating the granularity and physical packaging of software modules.
    - [Architecture Styles](.roo/vendor/architecture/fundamentals-of-software-architecture-mark-richards/09-architecture-styles.md): Software architecture design, system topology analysis, evaluating the transition from monolithic to distributed systems, and addressing integration, networking, and structural trade-offs in software development.
    - [Foundations](.roo/vendor/architecture/fundamentals-of-software-architecture-mark-richards/10-foundations.md): Analyzing, designing, refactoring, or extending architecture topologies; extracting components into remote services; writing inter-service communication logic (APIs, REST, RPC, messaging); implementing database transactions across multiple domains; or setting up system observability (logging).
    - [Layered Architecture Style](.roo/vendor/architecture/fundamentals-of-software-architecture-mark-richards/11-layered-architecture-style.md): Layered Architecture (also known as n-tiered architecture style), a monolithic architecture style where components are organized into logical horizontal layers, each performing a specific technical role (e.g., presentation, business logic, persistence, and database).
    - [Pipeline Architecture Style](.roo/vendor/architecture/fundamentals-of-software-architecture-mark-richards/12-pipeline-architecture-style.md): Designing, analyzing, or refactoring data processing workflows, ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) pipelines, EDI (Electronic Data Interchange) systems, task orchestrators/mediators (e.g., Apache Camel), or any architecture explicitly referred to as "Pipeline Architecture" or "Pipes and Filters" style.
    - [Microkernel Architecture Style](.roo/vendor/architecture/fundamentals-of-software-architecture-mark-richards/13-microkernel-architecture-style.md): Microkernel Architecture (also known as a "Plug-in Architecture"): A monolithic architecture style consisting of a core system and independent plug-in components, designed for high extensibility, adaptability, and the isolation of highly volatile, custom, or client-specific processing logic.
    - [Service-Based Architecture Style](.roo/vendor/architecture/fundamentals-of-software-architecture-mark-richards/14-service-based-architecture-style.md): Service-Based Architecture: A pragmatic, distributed architecture style consisting of a separately deployed user interface, a small number (typically 4 to 12) of separately deployed coarse-grained remote domain services, and a monolithic centrally shared database, designed to provide architectural modularity without the high complexity, cost, and orchestration overhead of fine-grained microservices.
    - [Event-Driven Architecture Style](.roo/vendor/architecture/fundamentals-of-software-architecture-mark-richards/15-event-driven-architecture-style.md): Analyzing, designing, or implementing distributed asynchronous systems, event-driven architectures (EDA), messaging systems, message brokers, event mediators, publish-and-subscribe models, asynchronous workflows, or when assessing trade-offs between request-based and event-based models.
    - [Space-Based Architecture Style](.roo/vendor/architecture/fundamentals-of-software-architecture-mark-richards/16-space-based-architecture-style.md): Space-Based Architecture (SBA): Architectural design for systems requiring extreme scalability, high elasticity, high performance, and the ability to handle variable or unpredictable concurrent user volumes, typically utilizing tuple spaces or in-memory data grids.
    - [Orchestration-Driven Service-Oriented Architecture](.roo/vendor/architecture/fundamentals-of-software-architecture-mark-richards/17-orchestration-driven-service-oriented-architecture.md): Orchestration-Driven Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)
    - [Microservices Architecture](.roo/vendor/architecture/fundamentals-of-software-architecture-mark-richards/18-microservices-architecture.md): Microservices Architecture, distributed systems decoupled by domain, bounded contexts, microfrontends, or decentralized service communication topologies.
    - [Choosing the Appropriate Architecture Style](.roo/vendor/architecture/fundamentals-of-software-architecture-mark-richards/19-choosing-the-appropriate-architecture-style.md): Designing a new software architecture, selecting or recommending an architecture style, planning an architectural migration, or performing architectural trade-off analysis for a specified problem domain.
    - [Techniques and Soft Skills](.roo/vendor/architecture/fundamentals-of-software-architecture-mark-richards/20-techniques-and-soft-skills.md): Software architecture design, architectural decision-making, team facilitation and leadership simulation, architecture risk analysis, generating documentation/diagrams for architectural topologies, evaluating technology stacks, and defining software development team constraints and checklists.
    - [Architecture Decisions](.roo/vendor/architecture/fundamentals-of-software-architecture-mark-richards/21-architecture-decisions.md): The domain involves evaluating, proposing, documenting, or updating technology choices, architectural structures, system designs, coding standards, or any decisions impacting a system's non-functional characteristics, specifically focusing on the creation, review, or modification of Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) and identifying architecturally significant choices.
    - [Analyzing Architecture Risk](.roo/vendor/architecture/fundamentals-of-software-architecture-mark-richards/22-analyzing-architecture-risk.md): Analyzing software architecture risk, evaluating architectural diagrams or proposals for vulnerabilities, assessing Agile project/story risk, conducting risk storming sessions, evaluating new technology choices, or generating risk assessment reports.
    - [Diagramming and Presenting Architecture](.roo/vendor/architecture/fundamentals-of-software-architecture-mark-richards/23-diagramming-and-presenting-architecture.md): The domain encompasses generating, reviewing, or structuring architecture diagrams (e.g., PlantUML, Mermaid, Graphviz), structural design documents, presentation slide decks (e.g., Reveal.js, Markdown slides, PowerPoint outlines), or infodecks.
    - [Making Teams Effective](.roo/vendor/architecture/fundamentals-of-software-architecture-mark-richards/24-making-teams-effective.md): The AI acting in a software architect capacity managing project governance, defining team workflows, creating developer guidelines, reviewing code completions, establishing project structures, managing dependencies/third-party libraries, or evaluating team dynamics, task assignments, and repository conflict resolution.
    - [Negotiation and Leadership Skills](.roo/vendor/architecture/fundamentals-of-software-architecture-mark-richards/25-negotiation-and-leadership-skills.md): This rule set is activated whenever the AI is engaged in architectural decision-making, evaluating design trade-offs, resolving technical disputes, proposing refactoring efforts, or guiding the user/development team through the implementation of an architecture.
    - [Developing a Career Path](.roo/vendor/architecture/fundamentals-of-software-architecture-mark-richards/26-developing-a-career-path.md): Software architecture career development, continuous learning, technology assessment, skill diversification, maintaining technical breadth, building a personal technology radar, and practicing architecture exercises (e.g., Katas).
